message.content:
The air grows thin, a held breath in the pines,
As twilight bleeds to indigo and gray.
The wind, a ghost, forgets its ancient lines,
And drops its voice to hush the dying day.
Above the ridge, the clouds begin to tear,
Releasing secrets kept since summer's heat,
A silent, silver, slow-descending prayer,
That turns the world to something soft and sweet.

First, one flake lands upon the chimney stone,
A tiny star extinguished by the cold.
Then another, drifting, light as bone,
To join the first, a story yet untold.
The village sleeps beneath a quilt of blue,
Its wooden roofs, its fences, and its gate,
Waiting for the sky to fall on through,
To seal the earth in winter's gentle weight.

The church bell tolls, a muffled, distant sound,
As white begins to blur the sharp-edged trees.
The river slows, its current turning round,
And wraps itself in lace of frozen breeze.
No footstep breaks the silence on the street,
No lantern swings to mark the passing hour;
Only the snow, with patient, soft retreat,
Covers the dust of every passing shower.

It piles upon the thatch of Miller's barn,
A thick, white fur against the fading light.
It fills the hollows of the garden lawn,
And turns the day into a dream of night.
The children's sleds, forgotten in the shed,
Will soon be pulled into the waiting drift,
But now, the village lies in quiet bed,
As if the world has paused, and ceased to shift.

The moon rises, a pearl in velvet deep,
And casts a glow on fields of endless white.
The shadows stretch
